## Real Material - Architect Learning Build (Database: Relational Databases)

This section is the study-first architecture guide for rds, aurora, proxies, replication, and relational workload design.

### Phase 1 - Foundations

#### Module: Relational Databases Mental Model
- Use relational databases decisions to shape blast radius, access paths, and operational ownership.
- Study service behavior first, then map it into production architecture and exam-ready design language.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- Just instead of connecting to your RDS database instance or your Aurora database, you'd now just connect to your RDS Proxy and you are done.
- And therefore your Lambda functions will have a read-only endpoint that is having the same feature of RDS Proxy but for your Read Replicas.
- But on top of it, RDS proxy for Aurora has the ability to create a RDS proxy only for the read-only endpoints of Aurora.
- So the RDS Proxy supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Microsoft SQL Server, as well as Aurora for MySQL and PostgreSQL.
- The failover times for Aurora and for RDS databases can be reduced by up to 66% by using RDS proxy.
- Now, the other option is to create an actual Aurora Read Replica on your RDS database instance.
- So it is completely possible to create an Aurora Read Replica on an RDS database instance.
- Alternatively, if you do want to manage connections, you have RDS Proxy for Aurora.
- So what this means that if you have a RDS DB instance in us-east-1a and then a Read Replica in us-east-1b, and there is asynchronous replication because this is a Read Replica, even though the traffic goes from one AZ to a different AZ that replication traffic is going to be free because RDS is a managed service and they give you that traffic for free.
- This is going to point directly into the master Aurora database, and then the client, if it talks to the write endpoint, will be redirected to that master, and then we have a bunch of read replicas and they could be autoscaling as well, so it's possible for you to add and remove read replicas over time.
- So all these are a bunch of options that we can use, but as we can see, the whole power of RDS is to manage database for us to have read replicas, multi-AZ, you know, we can increase the instance type as we go along and so on and that's all comes very handy from a managed service perspective.
- But if you are using a cross region replica, so you are in one region, us-east-1 and you go to another region, eu-west-1, for example then your RDS DB instance and your Read Replica will have replication that will go across regions and this will incur a replication fee for your network.

#### Module: Operational and Architecture Patterns
- Patterns matter when scale, governance, or reliability turns basic service use into system design.
- Prefer design implications and trade-offs over memorizing isolated features.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- So the RDS Proxy supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Microsoft SQL Server, as well as Aurora for MySQL and PostgreSQL.
- And so it's possible for you to create an RDS proxy in front of that primary instance, and then have a traditional application architecture; for example, the users talk to the application load balancer, which talks to the Lambda function, and then the RDS proxy.
- So now that we've talked about traditional database architecture with RDS as our example, let's start to shift gears and talk about a more scalable and more massively scalable solution with a NoSQL database.
- So the use cases for Aurora are the same as RDS, but you have less maintenance, more flexibility, more performance, and more features out of the box, which makes Aurora a great choice.
- Going into the exam is going to be extremely important to understand the difference between RDS Read Replicas and Multi AZ and understand exactly the use cases for those.
- And by doing a lot of optimization and smart stuff, basically they get 5x performance improvements over MySQL on the RDS, or 3x extra performance of PostgreSQL on RDS.
- So as you're designing your Aurora cluster, you want to be sure to choose the instance class that matches up with your use case and your performance requirements.
- RDS stands for Relational Database Service, and what it means is that it's a managed database service for database that use SQL as a query language.
- If I need to scale, if I need to improve the performance of the RDS instance, then I'll use something like read replicas to accomplish that.
- Just instead of connecting to your RDS database instance or your Aurora database, you'd now just connect to your RDS Proxy and you are done.
- And therefore your Lambda functions will have a read-only endpoint that is having the same feature of RDS Proxy but for your Read Replicas.
- And read replicas are going to provide read only copies of our RDS instance to improve the performance and also to improve durability.

### Phase 2 - Core Services
- Amazon Aurora should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon RDS should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon CloudWatch should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon EC2 should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- AWS SAM should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.

### Phase 3 - Advanced Patterns
- Pattern emphasis: Caching and Latency Reduction becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Database Operational Context becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Database Operations and Resilience becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: NoSQL Access Patterns becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Relational Consistency and HA becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.

### Phase 4 - System Design (Production)
#### Reference Architecture
- Design relational databases with explicit service boundaries, operational controls, and recovery expectations.
#### Scaling Considerations
- Scaling risk appears when relational databases decisions are made service-by-service without a whole-system operating model.
#### Cost Considerations
- The main cost driver is usually misalignment between service choice, operations, and workload behavior.

## Hands-On Practice (Kid-Friendly Step-by-Step)

### Safety First
- Use a sandbox account only.
- Set a budget alert before creating resources.
- Delete resources after practice to avoid surprise cost.

### Service-Specific Lab Paths
### Activity 1: Amazon RDS Lab for RDS, Aurora, proxies, replication, and relational workload design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> RDS -> Databases
- Azure equivalent: Azure SQL Database
- GCP equivalent: Cloud SQL
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> RDS -> Databases.
3. Build: Create a small dev database instance.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Check endpoint, connect test, and run one query.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete DB instance and snapshots not needed.

### Activity 2: Amazon DynamoDB Lab for RDS, Aurora, proxies, replication, and relational workload design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> DynamoDB -> Tables
- Azure equivalent: Azure Cosmos DB
- GCP equivalent: Firestore
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> DynamoDB -> Tables.
3. Build: Create a table with partition key and add one item.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Query the item using the partition key.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete test table.

### Activity 3: Amazon S3 Lab for RDS, Aurora, proxies, replication, and relational workload design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> S3 -> Buckets
- Azure equivalent: Azure Blob Storage
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Storage
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> S3 -> Buckets.
3. Build: Create a bucket and upload one sample file.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Open object metadata and test download.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete objects and delete bucket.

### Activity 4: AWS DMS Lab for RDS, Aurora, proxies, replication, and relational workload design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> DMS
- Azure equivalent: Azure Database Migration Service
- GCP equivalent: Database Migration Service
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> DMS.
3. Build: Create source and target endpoints and one migration task.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Validate task status and replicated rows.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete migration task and endpoints.

### Quick Troubleshooting
- Access denied: verify role permissions or ask admin for least-privilege lab policy.
- Resource limit reached: pick another region or request quota increase.
- Unexpected charges: stop/delete resources and review cost explorer immediately.

## Cross-Cloud Thinking Mode

Use this lens to study RDS, Aurora, proxies, replication, and relational workload design in a cloud-agnostic way: focus on capability first, provider name second.

### Capability Mapping (AWS / Azure / GCP)
| Capability | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identity | IAM / IAM Identity Center | Microsoft Entra ID + RBAC | Cloud IAM |
| Object Storage | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| Virtual Machines | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| Serverless Functions | AWS Lambda | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions / Cloud Run |
| Containers | Amazon EKS / ECS | AKS | GKE / Cloud Run |
| Databases | RDS / DynamoDB / ElastiCache | Azure SQL / Cosmos DB / Azure Cache | Cloud SQL / Firestore / Memorystore |
| Networking | VPC / Route 53 / ELB | VNet / DNS / Load Balancer | VPC / Cloud DNS / Cloud Load Balancing |
| Observability | CloudWatch / X-Ray / CloudTrail | Azure Monitor / App Insights / Activity Log | Cloud Monitoring / Cloud Logging / Cloud Trace |

### Same Lab Across 3 Clouds
| Lab Theme | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amazon RDS | Amazon RDS | Azure SQL Database | Cloud SQL |
| Amazon DynamoDB | Amazon DynamoDB | Azure Cosmos DB | Firestore |
| Amazon S3 | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| AWS DMS | AWS DMS | Azure Database Migration Service | Database Migration Service |

### How To Think Cross-Cloud
1. Start with workload requirement: latency, throughput, security, and compliance.
2. Map requirement to capability family (identity, network, compute, data, observability).
3. Compare service behavior, not branding: limits, scaling model, and failure modes.
4. Keep a portable architecture note: what is generic vs provider-specific.
5. Test same mini-lab in at least 2 clouds and write down differences.

### Child-Friendly Practice Challenge
- Pick one simple task (for example: upload one file, run one function, read one metric).
- Do the same task in AWS, Azure, and GCP.
- Fill this mini template:
  - What was the same?
  - What had a different name?
  - What felt easier/harder and why?

### File: outputs/AWS Storage and DB/awsdatabase/02_RDS/009_RDS Backups and Snapshots.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In this video, we'll learn how backups and snapshots work.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We're going to explore both automated backups and manual snapshots.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And the automated backups are going to occur every single day.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: This is done by taking a storage volume snapshot of your RDS instance, and in the background it's stored in S3.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The backup is taken during the backup window that you specify, and you get free backup storage included with your database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now you only get enough storage space that is equal to the size of your database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So if you store more than one backup, you're going to start to incur costs for the storage of those backups.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So just understand that as you choose your retention policy, longer retention policies are going to result in increased cost.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But yeah, you can specify the retention policies that you want, and you'll be able to restore your database to any point in time during that retention policy.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the default retention policy is it will keep seven daily backups one week worth of backups.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can set the retention policy to a maximum of 35 days, and you'll be able to restore to any point in time over the course of those 35 days.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience, Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: When you delete an RDS instance, the automated backups are also deleted.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Once they're deleted, they cannot be recovered.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: Manual snapshots are still retained, but you'll see this process when we demonstrate how to delete an RDS instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Automated backups run during the backup window that you've specified, and as the backup is being taken, there is going to be a brief IO suspension.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: Now that's only the case if we are not doing a multi-az RDS deployment.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We haven't learned about those yet, but with Multi-az, you're going to have a primary instance and a standby.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In that case, the backup is taken from the standby.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So there's no impact on the performance of the primary.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you choose to set the retention policy to zero, that is going to disable automated backups.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you then choose to change that later on from 0 to 1 or to some other retention policy, then at that point a backup is going to be taken at that exact moment.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you choose to disable automated backups, all of your automated backups will be deleted and cannot be recovered.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But one of the key features of the automated backups is it gives you point in time recovery.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It gives you the ability to recover to any point in time during the retention period of those backups, down to the second, so you can pick the time that you want to restore from if you need to restore from an automated backup.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Manual snapshots are different.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can back up your database instance manually at any time by taking a full database snapshot.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is a full backup.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It is not incremental, meaning every time you do this, it's going to create a complete copy of your database instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's going to take a snapshot of the storage volume.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It doesn't just backup individual databases.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It backs up every single database that's stored with that instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And these manual snapshots are kept until you explicitly delete them.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They're not deleted when you terminate an instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, much like the automated backups, taking a manual snapshot causes a brief IO suspension usually lasts no more than a few minutes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: And again, if you've enabled multi-az RDS, the snapshot is taken from the standby and therefore there's no impact on IO.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Any time you restore, whether it's from a manual snapshot or an automated backup, it doesn't matter.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience, Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: Any restore is always done to a new RDS instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And because these manual snapshots are a point in time picture, because it's a full backup at the moment that you take that snapshot, it does not support point in time recovery.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can't choose the minute or the second that you want to restore to.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You're just restoring to whenever that snapshot was taken.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 80:

- Concepts: Database Operational Context

Line 17:

Line 18:

- Services: (none explicit)

Line 19:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

Line 20:

Line 21:

Line 22:

Line 23:

Line 27:

Line 1:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: Amazon RDS

Line 2:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's get started with the Read Replicas.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Read Replicas as the name indicates help you to scale your reads.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's have an example.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: Here, we have our application and we have an RDS database instance and our application performs reads and writes to our database instance, but say we want to scale the reads because that's, main database instance can not scale enough.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It receives too many requests.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Well, we can create up to 15 Read Replicas and they can be within the same availability zone, cross availability zone, or cross region.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So three different options and they're very important to remember.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So say we have another RDS instance Read Replica and another one and what will happen is that they will be an asynchronous replication between the main RDS database instance and the two Read Replicas.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Asynchronous, that means that the reads are eventually consistent.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What that means is that, for example, if your application reads from the Read Replica before they had the chance to replicate the data then you may get all data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this is why it's called eventually consistent asynchronous replication.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And these replicas, they can be awesome for reading for scaling reads but they can also be promoted to their own database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you can take one of these replicas and say, okay I want it to be its own database now and take rights.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so you promote it to its own database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: It's completely out of the replication mechanism after that but it's lives and has its own life cycle afterwards.

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

Line 25:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: And so if you plug in that reporting application onto the main RDS database instance then it's going to overload it and possibly slow down the production application and you don't want that.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So instead what you do as a solutions architect is you create a Read Replica to run the new workload there.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you create a Read Replica.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Database Operational Context

Line 32:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

Line 35:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

Line 38:

Line 39:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So what this means that if you have a RDS DB instance in us-east-1a and then a Read Replica in us-east-1b, and there is asynchronous replication because this is a Read Replica, even though the traffic goes from one AZ to a different AZ that replication traffic is going to be free because RDS is a managed service and they give you that traffic for free.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 43:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 45:

Line 2:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon Aurora, Amazon RDS

Line 3:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For this, you will be having the master and any replica encrypted using KMS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this is defined at launch time during the first launch of your database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If somehow you haven't encrypted the master database, the main database, then the read replicas cannot be encrypted.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Consistency and failover signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, if you wanted to encrypt an already existing unencrypted database, what you would have to do is to take a database snapshot from that un-encrypted database, and then you restore that database snapshot as an encrypted database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Okay?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you have to go through a snapshot and restore operation.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is for at-rest encryption.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you have in-flight encryption.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So between your clients and your database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon Aurora, Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So each database on RDS and Aurora is ready to have in-flight encryption by default.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so therefore, your clients must use the TLS root certificates from AWS.

Line 15:

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA

Line 18:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 19:

- Services: Amazon EC2

Line 20:

Line 21:

Line 104:

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA, Selection and Trade-off Analysis

Line 4:

Line 5:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS

Line 7:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It receives too many requests.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Well, we can create up to 15 Read Replicas and they can be within the same availability zone, cross availability zone, or cross region.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So three different options and they're very important to remember.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So say we have another RDS instance Read Replica and another one and what will happen is that they will be an asynchronous replication between the main RDS database instance and the two Read Replicas.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Asynchronous, that means that the reads are eventually consistent.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What that means is that, for example, if your application reads from the Read Replica before they had the chance to replicate the data then you may get all data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this is why it's called eventually consistent asynchronous replication.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And these replicas, they can be awesome for reading for scaling reads but they can also be promoted to their own database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you can take one of these replicas and say, okay I want it to be its own database now and take rights.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so you promote it to its own database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS

- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So in case you wanna use Read Replicas, the main application in orange in the top of the screen must update the connection string to leverage the list of all the Read Replicas you have in your RDS cluster.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 22:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context

Line 23:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS

Line 24:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 25:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: And so if you plug in that reporting application onto the main RDS database instance then it's going to overload it and possibly slow down the production application and you don't want that.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 27:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you create a Read Replica.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: There is some asynchronous, a replication that happens between your main RDS DB database instance and your Read Replica and then your reporting application can just do reads from your Read Replica and run the analytics there.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The production application is completely unaffected in this case and that's perfect.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So remember, if you have a Read Replica you need to ensure that it is only for SELECT type of statements and SELECT is a SQL keyword.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context

### File: outputs/aws-certified-solutions-architect-associate-saa-c03/09_AWS Fundamentals_ RDS + Aurora + ElastiCache/099_ElastiCache for Solution Architects.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction, Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: Now, let's talk about ElastiCache Security.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: So ElastiCache supports IAM authentication for Redis only and for the rest you just use a username and password.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Access-pattern design signal.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction, Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: If you define IAM policies on ElastiCache, they're only used for AWS API-level security.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Access-pattern design signal; Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction, Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you have security within Redis called Redis AUTH, where you can set a password and a token when you create a Redis cluster, which gives you an extra level of security for your cache on top of your security groups.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-pattern design signal; Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It also supports SSL in flight encryption.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next we have Memcached.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-pattern design signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So Memcached supports SASL-based authentication which is quite an advanced mechanism.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-pattern design signal.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Just remember, just a name.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction, Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So to summarize, when we have an EC2 instance in a client, for example, we could use Redis AUTH to connect to Redis Cluster that's protected by Redis Security Group and we can have in-flight encryption.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Or alternatively, we could, for example, leverage IAM authentication for Redis.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: Now, there are three kinds of patterns for loading data into ElastiCache.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-pattern design signal.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: The number one is Lazy Loading, where all the read data is cached and data can become stale in the cache, Write Through, which is to add or update data in the cache whenever it is written to a database where there's no stale data, and using ElastiCache as a session store that we saw before and then we can expire a session using the time to live features.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Access-pattern design signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, caching is very hard and there's this very famous quote in computer science that "There are only two hard things in computer science: caching invalidation and naming things." So caching is a very complicated topic and give you a very high-level overview, but let's illustrate the lazy loading strategy here.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: So your application, if it has a cache hit in ElastiCache data from the cache, otherwise there's a cache miss, therefore, you read from the database and you write to the cache.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-pattern design signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: Amazon ElastiCache
- Key Insights: This is something we've seen from before and it is called lazy loading because only when you don't have a cache hit, then you load the data into Amazon ElastiCache.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Access-pattern design signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction, Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about the use cases for Redis and there's one you need to know going to the exam.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It is around creating a gaming leaderboard.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes db behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context

Line 20:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 21:

Line 22:

Line 23:
- Concepts: Caching and Latency Reduction

Line 20:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So we have a Master RDS Database, in one availability zone, and it's going to be synchronously replicated to another standby instance, in another AZ, so, AZ B.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: Your application only accesses your RDS database through one DNS name, and that DNS name will change automatically in case of a failover.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Consistency and failover signal.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so that means that your standby database is never accessed by your application, only your master database is, and there will be reads and writes happening directly, solely onto the master instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So here, multi AZ is really for recovery in case of outage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Database Operations and Resilience
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's a backup database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational database guardrail signal.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's a standby database, but our application never uses it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And if we wanted to scale the reads, then we would use read replicas.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So these read replicas are going to be asynchronous replication, so we have eventual consistency, and they can be created cross-region.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Database Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So it's possible to use replica to reduce the latencies in different regions in AWS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Relational Consistency and HA
- Services: AWS SAM, Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So your RDS instance will have an asynchronous replication into another RDS read replica, and then, again, same region or different region.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Consistency and failover signal.

Line 30:

## Step 2 - Consolidation

### 1. Concepts List
- Caching and Latency Reduction
- Database Operational Context
- Database Operations and Resilience
- NoSQL Access Patterns
- Relational Consistency and HA
- Selection and Trade-off Analysis
- Specialized Data Models

### 2. Services List
- AWS Batch
- AWS CloudFormation
- AWS CloudTrail
- AWS Config
- AWS Database Migration Service
- AWS Lambda
- AWS SAM
- Amazon Aurora
- Amazon CloudWatch
- Amazon Comprehend
- Amazon Data Firehose
- Amazon DynamoDB
- Amazon EBS
- Amazon EC2
- Amazon ECR
- Amazon ECS
- Amazon ElastiCache
- Amazon EventBridge
- Amazon Glacier
- Amazon Kinesis
- Amazon Lex
- Amazon OpenSearch
- Amazon RDS
- Amazon S3
- Auto Scaling
- Elastic Load Balancing
- SageMaker

### 3. Features List
- aurora
- dynamodb
- elasticache
- proxy
- rds

### 4. Use Cases

## Step 3 - Deep Expansion (Database Concepts)

### Core Services in This Topic
- AWS Batch: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- AWS CloudFormation: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- AWS CloudTrail: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- AWS Config: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- AWS Database Migration Service: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- AWS Lambda: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- AWS SAM: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- Amazon Aurora: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- Amazon CloudWatch: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.
- Amazon Comprehend: understand what it is, why it exists, its control plane, and the design trade-offs it introduces.

### Architect-Level Concept Expansion
- Caching and Latency Reduction: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.
- Database Operational Context: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.
- Database Operations and Resilience: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.
- NoSQL Access Patterns: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.
- Relational Consistency and HA: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.
- Selection and Trade-off Analysis: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.
- Specialized Data Models: treat this as a design lever that changes topology, risk posture, or operational workflow.

## Cross-Cloud Mapping (Concept-First)

| Underlying Concept | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Managed relational database | RDS / Aurora | Azure SQL / PostgreSQL / MySQL | Cloud SQL / AlloyDB |
| Key-value NoSQL | DynamoDB | Cosmos DB / Table API | Firestore / Bigtable |
| Managed cache | ElastiCache / MemoryDB | Azure Cache for Redis | Memorystore |
| Document database | DocumentDB | Cosmos DB / MongoDB-compatible services | MongoDB Atlas / Firestore document patterns |
| Graph and time-series | Neptune / Timestream | Cosmos graph / Data Explorer time-series patterns | Neo4j / Bigtable / time-series patterns |
| Cassandra-compatible managed service | Keyspaces | Managed Cassandra / Cosmos Cassandra API | Managed Cassandra on GCP ecosystems |

### Trade-offs and Decision Notes
- Database Knowledge Base is strongest when design decisions are expressed as control objectives rather than vendor-only terminology.
- Cross-cloud portability improves when service choice, failure behavior, and governance model are documented together.
- Cloud-native implementations should still preserve comparable intent across AWS, Azure, and GCP.

## Phase-wise Architect-Level Organization

### Phase 1 - Foundations
- Database architecture follows workload shape: relational, key-value, cache, document, graph, or time-series.
- Durability, consistency, and scaling trade-offs must be explicit at design time.

### Phase 2 - Core Services
- Implement replication, indexing, and latency controls based on real access patterns, not vendor labels.
- Operational safety depends on backup, restore, security, and connection management discipline.

### Phase 3 - Advanced Patterns
- Use caching and specialized engines deliberately to isolate hot paths from persistence concerns.
- Selection quality improves when trade-offs are documented as failure behavior, not feature checklists.

### Phase 4 - System Design
- Reference architecture: transactional core, access-optimized accelerators, and operational recovery workflows.
- Scaling considerations: partition balance, replica lag, connection storms, and hot key risk.
- Cost considerations: always-on relational sizing, cache duplication, and overfitted specialized engines.
